24.10: bāʿēt hahîʾ ʿālû ʿaḇdê nᵉḇûḵaḏneʾṣṣar — at that time the servants of Nebuchadnezzar came up — bāʿēt hahîʾ (at that time) ties the Babylonian siege to the reign of Jehoiachin, the narrator holding timeline and theological judgment together. The plural ʿāḇdê (servants) — Nebuchadnezzar’s subordinates arrive first, before the king himself (v11). The city is put under siege — wayyābōʾ hāʿîr bammāṣôr — the word māṣôr (siege/siege-work) carries military and legal weight: the siege is the covenant curse of Deut 28:52-57 enacted in history.
24.11: ûnᵉḇûḵaḏneʾṣṣar melek bāḇel bāʾ ʿal hāʿîr — Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon came to the city — the king himself arrives, elevating the personal weight of the confrontation. The grammar puts Nebuchadnezzar in parallel with David’s capture of Jerusalem from the Jebusites (2 Sam 5:6-9): as David came against the city and took it, Nebuchadnezzar comes against David’s city. The reversal is total: the city won by the first David is being taken by the king who will end the Davidic political order. wayyiṣbᵉʾû ʿālayw ʿăḇāḏāyw — his servants were besieging it — the pluperfect-sequential indicating that the servants’ siege was already underway when the king arrived.
24.12: wayyēṣēʾ yᵉhôyāḵîn melek yᵉhûḏāh — Jehoiachin king of Judah went out — the verb yāṣāʾ (went out) for voluntary surrender is the exact opposite of the king’s expected role of defending or fleeing. ʿal melek bāḇel hûʾ wᵉʾimmô wᵉʿaḇāḏāyw wᵉśārāyw wᵉsārîsāyw — to the king of Babylon, he and his mother and his servants and his princes and his officials — the entire court surrenders en masse; the vocabulary itemizes the covenant-community of the king (mother, servants, princes, officials). The specific note of the king’s mother (ʾimmô) is significant: the queen-mother in Judah holds a formal institutional role, and her surrender marks the end of that office’s Davidic iteration.
24.13: wayyōṣēʾ miššām ʾet kol ʾôṣrôt bêt YHWH wᵉʾôṣrôt bêt hammelek — he took from there all the treasuries of the house of YHWH and the treasuries of the king’s house — the stripping of the temple treasuries is narrated with the same verb yāṣāʾ (went out, brought out) used for the king’s surrender in v12; both the king and the treasure are ‘brought out’ to Babylon. wayyᵉqaṣṣēṣ ʾet kol kᵉlê hazzāhāḇ — he cut in pieces all the vessels of gold — qāṣaṣ (cut to pieces) is a term of violent reduction; the gold vessels made by Solomon for worship are dismembered. The narrator cites Solomon’s specific decree (ʾăšer ʿāśāh šᵉlōmōh melek yiśrāʾēl bᵉhêḵal YHWH kaʾăšer dibbēr YHWH) — as YHWH had spoken — invoking the fulfillment of the prophetic word about the temple’s despoliation (1 Kings 9:7-9).
24.15: wayyegeleh ʾet yᵉhôyāḵîn bāḇelāh — he exiled Jehoiachin to Babylon — the gālāh (exile/uncover) verb is the central lexeme of the exile narrative: deportation as uncovering/stripping, the opposite of YHWH’s covering of his people. The inventory of the deportees is precise: the king’s mother, wives, officials, and the leaders of the land (ʾêlê hāʾāreṣ — the powerful/mighty ones of the land). The word ʾêlê (terebinths/mighty ones) is a poetic term for the élite; the exile strips the land of its most powerful. Jehoiachin will be in Babylon for 37 years before the book’s closing scene of release (25:27).
24.16: wᵉkol ʾanšê hehayil šiḇʿat ʾălāpîm — all the men of valor, seven thousand — the military élite deported as a security measure, consistent with Babylonian imperial policy of decapitating captured nations by removing their fighting class. wehheḥārāš wᵉhammassēr ʾelep — the craftsmen and smiths, one thousand — the artisan class deported to serve Babylonian industry and to prevent the production of weapons in Judah. The deportation of skilled workers is documented in Assyrian and Babylonian administrative texts; the narrator uses the same categories. The two classes — warriors and craftsmen — are the embodiment of the covenant people’s civilizational capacity; their exile signals the end of Judah as a functioning state.
24.17: wayyamliḵ melek bāḇel ʾet mattanyāh dōḏô taḥtāyw — the king of Babylon made Mattaniah his uncle king in his place — the wayyamliḵ (causative: caused to reign) marks Babylonian installation of a client king; the sovereignty now rests with Nebuchadnezzar, not with the Davidic line itself. The name-change from Mattaniah to Zedekiah — wayyāsēb ʾet šᵉmô ṣiḏqiyyāhû — is a political act: renaming a vassal was a Near Eastern assertion of suzerainty (cf. the renaming of Daniel and his companions in Dan 1:7). The new name Ṣiḏqiyyāhû (YHWH is my righteousness) is theologically ironic: the king whose reign will end in covenant catastrophe bears a name that invokes covenantal fidelity.
24.18: ben ʿeśrîm wᵉʾaḥat šānāh ṣiḏqiyyāhû bᵉmolḵô ûšᵉtêʿeśrēh šānāh mālaḵ bîrûšālāim — standard regnal formula for Zedekiah: 21 years old, eleven-year reign. The eleven-year detail is significant: long enough to have fostered hope but ending in the complete destruction of ch25. The mother-name (wᵉšēm ʾimmô ḥamûṭal bat yirmᵉyāhû millibᵉnāh) connects Zedekiah to the same maternal line as Jehoahaz (23:31); both share the same mother from Libnah. The family genealogy is maintained in the formulaic record even as the kingdom collapses — the covenant accounting continues.
24.19: wayyaʿaś hārāʿ bᵉʿênê YHWH kᵉkōl ʾăšer ʿāśāh yᵉhôyāqîm — he did evil in the eyes of YHWH according to all that Jehoiakim had done — the covenant-evaluation formula aligning Zedekiah with his predecessor. The phrase kᵉkōl ʾăšer ʿāśāh (according to all that ... had done) creates a genealogy of apostasy: Manasseh’s wickedness becomes the benchmark (23:26), and subsequent kings are measured against it and against each other in a descending spiral. The imperfect verb ʿāśāh in the formula always describes a completed, evaluated pattern of behavior, not a single act.
24.20: kî ʿal ʾap YHWH hāyᵉtāh bîrûšālaim wîhûḏāh ʿaḏ hiššᵉlîḵô ʾōtām mēʿal pānāyw — for through the anger of YHWH it happened to Jerusalem and Judah until he had cast them out from his presence — the verse is the theological conclusion to ch24, interpreting the entire sequence of deportations and political disasters as the outworking of divine anger (ʾap, nose/anger — the somatic Hebrew idiom for intense emotion). The verb hiššᵉlîḵô (cast out) is the expulsion-from-presence verb used for Adam and Eve in Gen 3:23-24 (gāraš), establishing exile as the eschatological form of the garden expulsion. ûṣiḏqiyyāhû māraḏ bᵉmelek bāḇel — Zedekiah rebelled against the king of Babylon — māraḏ (rebel) setting up ch25’s catastrophe.

25.9: Nebuzaradan’s burning of the temple — wᵉʾet bêt YHWH wᵉʾet bêt hammelek wᵉʾēt kol bātê yᵉrûšālaim wᵉʾet kol bêt gāḏôl śārap bāʾēš, ‘he burned the house of YHWH and the king’s house and all the houses of Jerusalem; every great house he burned down’ — is the enacted fulfillment of the conditional covenant curse of 1 Kgs 9:7-9: wᵉhabbayit hazzeh yihyeh ʿelyôn, ‘this house will become a heap of ruins.’ The burning is not a historical accident but a covenant consequence: YHWH did exactly what he warned he would do. Lam 2:6-8 narrates the theological shock: YHWH himself is the destroyer — wayyaḥmōs kᵉgān śukkô šiḥēt môʿāḏô, ‘he has laid waste his booth like a garden, laid in ruins his place of meeting.’ Jer 7:14 had warned that calling out ‘the temple of YHWH’ was not protection: ‘I will do to the house that is called by my name, and in which you trust, and to the place that I gave to you and to your fathers, as I did to Shiloh.’ The burning fulfills what Jeremiah had said would be the final answer to false temple-confidence. Heb 9:26 identifies the NT resolution: Christ ‘has appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself’ — the temple that bore the sin of the people is gone; the sacrifice that finally addressed it has been made.
25.27: The final note of 2 Kings — nāśāʾ ʾet rōʾš yôyāḵîn melek yᵉhûḏāh mibêt keleʾ, ‘Evil-merodach king of Babylon, in the year he began to reign, lifted up the head of Jehoiachin king of Judah out of prison’ — ends the narrative on a deliberately open, ambiguous note. The idiom nāśāʾ ʾet rōʾš (to lift up the head) means to restore to honor (cf. Gen 40:13, where Pharaoh ‘lifts up the head’ of the cupbearer = restoration to office). Jehoiachin is released, given a throne, and fed at the king’s table for the rest of his life (vv28-30). He is not a free king — but he is alive, honored, and at the royal table. The Davidic line has not ended; the last king of Judah dies not in prison or in battle but at a king’s table. Von Rad reads this as the canonical signal of hope: the narrator ends not with the heap of ruins but with a Davidic heir alive. Matthew’s genealogy includes Jehoiachin (Iechonias, Matt 1:11-12) in the line from David to Jesus — the exile-king who was lifted up, honored, and whose seed continued, is placed in the chain that leads to the one whom God raises from the dead to a throne not in Babylon but at his right hand (Acts 2:33).
25.1: wayhî biššᵉnat haттᵉšîʿît lᵉmolḵô baḥōḏeš hāʿăśîrî ʿăśārāh laḥōḏeš — in the ninth year of his reign, in the tenth month, on the tenth day of the month — the narrator’s precise dating (year, month, day) elevates this event to the calendrical gravity of a feast or fast; indeed, this date becomes a fast day in the Judean calendar (Zech 8:19). bāʾ nᵉḇûḵaḏneʾṣṣar melek bāḇel hûʾ wᵉḵol ḥêlô — Nebuchadnezzar came, he and all his army — the personal and corporate arrival formula mirrors the formulaic language for divine theophany: the coming of the great king with his whole army. wayyiḥan ʿālayw wayyiḇnû ʿālayw dāyēq sāḇîḇ — he encamped against it and built a siege ramp around it — dāyēq (siege ramp/siege wall) is a loanword in some analyses; the siege-infrastructure vocabulary is technical military language.
25.2: wayyābōʾ hāʿîr bammāṣôr ʿad ʿaśtê ʿeśrēh šānāh — the city came under siege until the eleventh year of Zedekiah — the duration of the siege (roughly 18 months from the 9th to the 11th year) is recorded as the temporal frame of the catastrophe. The verb construction wayyābōʾ ... bammāṣôr (came ... under siege) presents the city itself as the subject, as if Jerusalem entered a new state. The length of the siege — survivable, protracted, grinding — is the form of covenant curse detailed in Deut 28:52-57: the siege that reduces the city to cannibalism and starvation before the final breach.
25.3: baḥōḏeš hārᵉḇîʿî bᵉtišʿāh laḥōḏeš wayyeḥezaq hārāʿāḇ bāʿîr — in the fourth month, on the ninth of the month, the famine was severe in the city — ḥāzaq (was severe/prevailed/was strong) is the same verb used for YHWH’s hand being strong against enemies; here the famine is the agent of YHWH’s covenant judgment, its severity expressed in the language of warfare. wᵉlōʾ hāyāh lehem lᵉʿam hāʾāreṣ — there was no bread for the people of the land — the covenant curse of Deut 28:17 (no grain in basket or kneading bowl) enacted. The phrase ʿam hāʾāreṣ (people of the land) here refers to the common population, distinct from the military and court élite who have already been deported.
25.4: tippāqaʿ hāʿîr — the city was breached — bāqaʿ (niphal: was split/breached) is the same verb used for the splitting of the Red Sea (Exod 14:21 in some forms) and for rivers splitting (Ps 78:13); the irony is total: the waters that split to save Israel at the Exodus now the walls of Jerusalem split to end the Davidic kingdom. wayyibrᵉḥû kol ʾanšê hammilḥāmāh hallaylāh — all the men of war fled at night — the night-flight echoes the Passover night and the escape from Egypt, but in reverse: Israel’s flight from Egypt was night-departure toward freedom; the flight from Jerusalem is night-departure toward destruction. The king goes ‘toward the Arabah’ (derek hāʿărāḇāh), fleeing toward the Jordan wilderness — the same wilderness through which Joshua had led Israel into the land.
25.5: wayyirḏᵉpû ḥêl kaśḏîm ʾaḥar hammelek wayyaśśîgû ʾōtô bᵉʿarᵉḇôt yᵉrēḥô — the Chaldean army pursued the king and overtook him in the plains of Jericho — the geographical precision is pointed: Jericho is the first city Israel conquered under Joshua; it is the city where the entry into the land began. The end of the Davidic kingdom occurs at the same place the covenant began to be embodied in land and conquest. wayyāpĕṣû mēʾalāyw kol ḥêlô — all his army scattered from him — the scattering of the shepherd’s army (Zech 13:7: ‘strike the shepherd, that the sheep may be scattered’) — the fulfillment of the Deuteronomic threat of military dissolution before enemies (Deut 28:25).
25.6: wayyitpᵉśû ʾet hammelek wayyaʿălû ʾōtô ʾel melek bāḇel riblātāh — they seized the king and brought him up to the king of Babylon at Riblah — the verb tāpaś (seized/apprehended) is used elsewhere for taking captives in war; the Davidic king is reduced to a prisoner of war. Riblah in the land of Hamath is the Babylonian headquarters in Syria — the same place where Pharaoh Necho had deposed Jehoahaz (23:33). Two Judean kings in succession are brought before foreign kings at Riblah; the legal venue underscores that Judah has become a subject people whose king must answer to a foreign court. wayyᵉdabbᵉrû ʾittô mišpāṭ — they pronounced judgment on him — mišpāṭ (judgment/sentence) using the covenant-law vocabulary for judicial verdict.
25.7: wᵉʾet bᵉnê ṣiḏqiyyāhû šāḥaṭû lᵉʿênāyw — they slaughtered Zedekiah’s sons before his eyes — šāḥaṭ (slaughter) is the term for the slaughter of animals for sacrifice; its application to the king’s sons is deliberately brutal, evoking ritual killing. The death of the sons before Zedekiah’s eyes is designed as the last thing he sees: wᵉʾet ʿênê ṣiḏqiyyāhû ʿiwwēr — and Zedekiah’s eyes they blinded — ʿiwwēr (piel: blinded) is the covenant-curse of Deut 28:28-29 (‘YHWH will strike you with madness, blindness...’). The last image Zedekiah sees is the death of his seed; he is then blinded so that this last image is, in a sense, his permanent vision. wayyaʾaśrēhû baněḥuštayim — they bound him in bronze shackles — nᵉḥuštayim (dual: bronze chains), possibly the same bronze that the Solomonic temple was adorned with, now used to bind its king.
25.8: ûḇaḥōḏeš haḥămiššî bᵉšiḇʿāh laḥōḏeš — in the fifth month, on the seventh day of the month — the precise date continues the calendrical weight of ch25; this date also becomes a fast day (Zech 7:3; 8:19). nᵉḇûzarʾaḏān rab ṭabbāḥîm — Nebuzaradan the captain of the guard — rab ṭabbāḥîm literally ‘chief of the slaughterers/executioners’; the same root as šāḥaṭ used for the slaughter of Zedekiah’s sons. The executioner-in-chief arrives to complete the work of destruction. ʿeḇeḏ melek bāḇel — servant of the king of Babylon — the title marking him as the foreign king’s delegate; in the narrative structure, this servant of Babylon’s king does what YHWH’s servants (the prophets) warned would happen if Israel did not return to YHWH’s service.
25.10: wᵉʾēt ḥômot yᵉrûšālaim sāḇîḇ nātᵉṣû kol ḥêl kaśḏîm — all the Chaldean army tore down the walls of Jerusalem around it — the destruction of the walls (ḥômāh) reverses the covenant symbol of the city’s protection: throughout the Psalms, YHWH was the wall and fortress of Zion (Ps 46:5; 48:12-13), and now the physical walls come down. The verb nāṭaṣ (tore down/broke down) is the same word used for the prophetic mandate to break down before building (Jer 1:10); here the breaking down is judgment, not preparation for renewal. The surrounding phrase sāḇîḇ (around/surrounding) echoes the siege language of v1: the army that surrounded the city now tears down what it surrounded.
25.11: wᵉʾēt yeter hāʿām hannišʾārîm bāʿîr — the rest of the people who remained in the city — the vocabulary of remnant (yeter, remainder; nišʾārîm, those left over) is heavily theological: the remnant that was not deported in the first two waves (ch24:14-16) is now also taken. wᵉʾet hahōlᵉlîm ʾăšer nāplû ʾel melek bāḇel — and the deserters who had gone over to the king of Babylon — nāpal ʾel (fell to/deserted to) is the verb for surrendering to an enemy king; those who had capitulated voluntarily are treated with the same exile as those who were captured, collapsing the distinction between resistance and surrender in the mechanics of empire.
25.12: ûmidallôt hāʾāreṣ hišʾîr rab ṭabbāḥîm lᵉkōrᵉmîm ûlᵉyōgᵉḇîm — from the poorest of the land the captain of the guard left some as vinedressers and farmers — dallôt (poorest/thin ones, from ḏal, weak/thin/poor) designates the agricultural underclass without military value. The Babylonian policy preserved the working poor to maintain the land’s agricultural productivity — a pragmatic imperial decision. The word kōrᵉmîm (vinedressers) evokes the vine-imagery of covenant blessing (Deut 8:8; Isa 5:1-7); the vine of YHWH’s people is tended now by the ‘poor’ left behind, the covenant community reduced to its bare subsistence minimum.
25.13: wᵉʾet ʿammûḏê hannᵉḥōšet ʾăšer bêt YHWH — the bronze pillars that were in the house of YHWH — the Chronicler begins the inventory of the temple furnishings with the pillars (Jachin and Boaz, 1 Kings 7:15-22); their bronze had been brought as spoil from David’s wars (2 Sam 8:8) and their names encoded covenant theology (‘he establishes’ and ‘in him is strength’). šibbar hakaśḏîm — the Chaldeans broke to pieces — šāḇar (broke) is used for the breaking of the covenant tablets (Exod 32:19), making the destruction of the temple furniture a visual counterpart of covenant rupture. wayyiśśᵉʾû ʾet nᵉḥuštām bāḇelāh — and they carried their bronze to Babylon — the movement of the temple metal reverses the movement of the Exodus spoils; what came out of Egypt as covenant wealth is now carried back eastward to an empire.
25.14: wᵉʾet haśśîrôt wᵉʾet hayya ʿîm wᵉʾet hamᵉzammᵉrôt — the pots, shovels, and snuffers — the inventory of smaller liturgical vessels continues the theological accounting of what the covenant community had accumulated for worship. The objects are all implements of the sacrificial system: pots for cooking sacrificial meat, shovels for removing ash, snuffers for the lampstand. wᵉʾēt kol kᵉlê hannᵉḥōšet ʾăšer yᵉšārᵉtû bāhem — all the bronze vessels with which they ministered — the verb šārat (minister/serve) is the Levitical service term; the objects of priestly ministry are stripped from the context of worship and carried to Babylon as plunder.
25.15: wᵉʾet hammaḥtôt wᵉʾet hammizrāqôt ʾăšer zāhāḇ zāhāḇ wᵉʾăšer kesep kesep — the firepans and bowls, whatever was of gold (took the gold ones), of silver (took the silver ones) — the repeated noun-noun construction (zāhāḇ zāhāḇ, gold gold; kesep kesep) is an emphatic distributive: every golden item, every silver item, was taken without exception. rab ṭabbāḥîm lāqāḥ — the captain of the guard took — the subject returns to Nebuzaradan as the agent of systematic despoliation; the temple’s wealth, accumulated through generations of Davidic kingship and Solomon’s construction program, is removed in a single systematic sweep.
25.16: hāʿammûḏîm šᵉnayim hayyām ʾeḥāḏ wᵉhammᵉkônôt ʾăšer ʿāśāh šᵉlōmōh lᵉḇêt YHWH — the two pillars, the one sea, and the stands that Solomon had made for the house of YHWH — the inventory returns to the largest objects, referencing Solomon’s original craftsmanship. The phrase lōʾ hāyāh mišqal linnᵉḥōšet kol hakkēlîm hāʾēlleh (the bronze of all these vessels was beyond weighing) is an echo of 1 Kings 7:47 where Solomon’s bronze work was ‘not weighed because it was too vast’ — what could not be weighed because of its abundance is now carried away because of the nation’s sin. The quantity that exceeded measurement in covenant flourishing now exceeds measurement as the object of judgment’s plunder.
25.17: šᵉmōneh ʿeśrēh ʾammāh qômāh hāʿammûḏ hāʾeḥāḏ — eighteen cubits the height of one pillar — the precise measurement (18 cubits ≈ 27 feet) reflects the narrator’s care to preserve the architectural memory of what was lost. The detailed description of the capital (the bronze kōteret, the pomegranates, the latticework) echoes the original construction account of 1 Kings 7:15-22 in miniature, as if the destruction narrative itself functions as an architectural memorial. The twelve pomegranates on the latticework — twelve suggesting the twelve tribes — mean that the destruction of the pillars is also the material end of the twelve-tribe covenant symbol. kēn lāʿammûḏ haššēnî ʿal haśśᵉḇāḵāh — so for the second pillar, with the latticework — the symmetry of the original construction is matched by the symmetry of its documentation at destruction.
25.18: wayyiqqaḥ rab ṭabbāḥîm ʾet śᵉrāyāh kōhēn hārōʾš wᵉʾet ṣᵉp̄anyāh kōhēn hammiššneh — the captain of the guard took Seraiah the chief priest and Zephaniah the second priest — the priestly leadership is deported alongside the political leadership; the covenant’s two institutional pillars (monarchy and priesthood) are both removed. ûšᵉlōšet šōmᵉrê hassap — and the three doorkeepers — the šōmᵉrê hassap (threshold guardians) are the Levitical gatekeepers of 2 Kings 12:9; their deportation ends the Levitical guard function at the threshold that had been maintained since the Deuteronomic reform. The doorkeepers of the covenant house are taken; there is no longer a threshold to guard.
25.19: ûmin hāʿîr lāqaḥ sārîs ʾeḥāḏ — from the city he took one official — the term sārîs (eunuch/official) designates royal court functionaries who managed palace administration. The itemized deportees in v19 — an official, five men of the king’s council (rōʾê pᵉnê hammelek, those who saw the king’s face — the idiom for inner-circle courtiers), the military secretary, sixty men of the land — represent a comprehensive sweep of the civil and military administrative class. The phrase rōʾê pᵉnê hammelek (those who see the king’s face) is a court-protocol idiom: access to the king’s face defined the hierarchy of intimacy; these are the innermost circle, and they are deported precisely because they are the most dangerous to leave behind.
25.20: wayyiqqaḥ ʾōtām nᵉḇûzarʾaḏān rab ṭabbāḥîm wayyōlēk ʾōtām ʾel melek bāḇel riblātāh — Nebuzaradan the captain of the guard took them and brought them to the king of Babylon at Riblah — the deportation of the priestly and civil leadership to Riblah for judgment creates a structural parallel with v6 (Zedekiah brought to Riblah) and with 23:33 (Jehoahaz brought to Riblah by Pharaoh Necho). Three times in 2 Kings a Judean leader or group is brought to Riblah for foreign judgment; the repetition establishes Riblah as the locus of covenant accountability that Jerusalem itself failed to be. The judgment that should have been executed in the king’s court in Jerusalem is now carried out in a foreign court in Syria.
25.21: wayyak ʾōtām melek bāḇel wayyᵉmîtēm bᵉriblāh bᵉʾereṣ ḥamāt — the king of Babylon struck them down and put them to death at Riblah in the land of Hamath — nākāh (struck) is the standard execution verb; mût (hiphil: caused to die/put to death) completes the judicial sentence. The death of the priests and officials at Riblah fulfills the prophetic warnings about the fate of those who led Israel astray (cf. Jer 52:24-27 parallels this passage closely). wayyigel yᵉhûḏāh mēʿal ʾaḏmātô — Judah was exiled from its land — the summary statement of the chapter’s theological verdict: gālāh (was exiled/went into exile), the verb from which ‘golah’ (exile) derives, expressing the stripping-away of the covenant people from their covenant land.
25.22: wᵉhāʿām hannišʾār bᵉʾereṣ yᵉhûḏāh — the people who remained in the land of Judah — nišʾār (remained/was left over) is the remnant vocabulary; the ‘remaining ones’ are those not deported, now to be governed by Gedaliah. wayyapqēḏ ʿălêhem nᵉḇûḵaḏneʾṣṣar melek bāḇel ʾet gᵉḏalyāhû ben ʾăḥîqām ben šāpān — Nebuchadnezzar appointed over them Gedaliah son of Ahikam son of Shaphan — the name Gedaliah (YHWH is great) and the lineage (Shaphan was the scribe who brought the lawbook to Josiah, 22:8; Ahikam protected Jeremiah, Jer 26:24) give Gedaliah strong covenant credentials. The appointment by the Babylonian king of a man from a family of covenant-fidelity is a small grace-note within the disaster narrative.
25.23: wayyišmᵉʿû kol śārê haḥăyālîm hēmmāh wᵉhāʾănāšîm — when all the captains of the forces heard, they and the men with them — the grammar marks the captains as the subject of the hearing: they receive the report and come to Gedaliah. The list of captains — Ishmael ben Netaniah, Johanan ben Kareah, Seraiah ben Tanhumeth the Netophathite, Jaazaniah son of the Maacathite — is a remnant military roster; these are the guerrilla commanders who had survived outside the city during the siege. Their coming to Gedaliah at Mizpah represents a potential reconstitution of Judean society around a non-Davidic but covenant-faithful administrator. hammiṣpātāh — to Mizpah — the place where Samuel had gathered Israel (1 Sam 7:5-12); Mizpah functions as an alternative covenant-assembly site when Jerusalem is unavailable.
25.24: wayyiššābaʿ lāhem gᵉḏalyāhû wᵉlāʾănāšêhem wayyōʾmer lāhem — Gedaliah swore to them and their men and said to them — nišbaʿ (sworn) marks a formal covenant-like assurance; Gedaliah’s oath to the captains is the basis for their settlement under his administration. ʾal tîrᵉʾû mēʿaḇdê kaśḏîm šᵉḇû bāʾāreṣ wᵉʿiḇdû ʾet melek bāḇel wᵉîṭaḇ lākem — do not be afraid of the servants of the Chaldeans; dwell in the land and serve the king of Babylon and it will go well with you — the imperative šᵉḇû (remain/dwell) activates the theology of the land: the covenant people should remain in the land even under foreign rule rather than flee (cf. Jer 42:10-12, the same counsel from Jeremiah). The ṭôḇ (good/well) echoes the covenant blessing of Deut 6:3, now mediated through a Babylonian client governor.
25.25: wayhî baḥōḏeš haššᵉḇîʿî — in the seventh month — another precise date marking the third disaster of ch25: assassination of Gedaliah. bāʾ yišmāʾēl ben nᵉtanyāh ben ʾĕlîšāmāʿ mizzaraʿ hammlûḵāh — Ishmael son of Netaniah son of Elishama of the royal seed — mizzaraʿ hammlûḵāh (of the royal seed) identifies Ishmael as a Davidide, making the assassination a dynastic murder: a member of the royal family kills the governor. wayyak ʾet gᵉḏalyāhû wayyāmōt — he struck Gedaliah and he died — the same verb-pair as v21 (the executions at Riblah); Gedaliah’s death by assassination mirrors the official executions of the deported leaders, compounding the disaster. The murder of the one remnant leader who represented covenant continuity destroys the last organized structure of Judean life in the land.
25.26: wayyāqom kol hāʿām miqqāṭōn wᵉʿaḏ gāḏôl wᵉśārê haḥăyālîm wayyāḇōʾû miṣrāyim — all the people, both small and great, and the captains of the forces arose and came to Egypt — miqqāṭōn wᵉʿaḏ gāḏôl (from small to great) is the covenant-totality formula indicating that the entire remaining community is included in the flight. The flight to Egypt is the ultimate covenant reversal: the Exodus had been from Egypt to Canaan; now the covenant people flee back to Egypt. The verb bōʾ miṣrāyim (came to Egypt) is the exact opposite of yāṣāʾ mimiṣrāyim (went out from Egypt) of the Exodus formula. kî yārᵉʾû mipĕnê kaśḏîm — for they were afraid of the Chaldeans — fear of the Babylonians drives them back to the land of bondage; the fear that the Exodus was meant to cure (YHWH as the one to fear) is replaced by fear of a human empire.
25.28: wayyᵉdabbēr ʾittô ṭōḇôt wayyittēn ʾet kissĕʾô mimmʿal kissēʾ hammlāḵîm ʾăšer ʾittô bāḇel — he spoke kindly with him and set his throne above the thrones of the kings who were with him in Babylon — dibbēr ṭōḇôt (spoke good things/kindly) is the vocabulary of grace and favor; the Babylonian king’s speech to Jehoiachin inverts Nebuchadnezzar’s earlier speech of judgment at Riblah. miмmaʿal kissēʾ (above the throne) — the elevation above other subject-kings is a formal honor in the Babylonian court hierarchy. The throne above thrones language is the grammar of the Davidic promise (Ps 89:27: ‘I will make him the firstborn, the highest of the kings of the earth’), here being approximated in Babylonian exile by a foreign king’s grace.
25.29: wᵉšinnāh ʾēt bigḏê kilĕʾô — he changed his prison garments — the changing of garments is a covenant-status marker throughout the OT: Joseph’s prison garments changed before Pharaoh (Gen 41:14), the leprous garment as mark of uncleanness (Lev 13), the high priest’s change of garments at Yom Kippur (Lev 16:23-24). Jehoiachin’s change from prison clothes to regular clothes is a form of ceremonial re-entry into the court community — a limited restoration of dignity without a restoration of power. wᵉʾāḵal leḥem lᵉpānāyw tāmîḏ kol yᵉmê ḥayyāyw — he ate bread before him continually all the days of his life — leḥem lᵉpānāyw (bread before him) is the idiom for eating at the king’s table, precisely the honor that Mephibosheth received from David (2 Sam 9:7, 10, 13): the covenant-kindness (ḥeseḏ) extended to the remnant of the previous dynasty.
25.30: wᵉʾaryōḥô tāmîḏ nittān lô mēʾet hammelek — and his regular allowance was given him by the king — ʾaryōḥô (his regular allowance/ration) is a hapax legomenon in the OT, possibly related to an Akkadian term for a food ration; archaeological tablets from Babylon (the Weidner tablets) mention rations for ‘Yaukin king of Judah’ and his sons, providing extra-biblical confirmation of this narrative. dᵉḇar yôm bᵉyômô — a daily portion for each day — the same formula used for the daily bread of the wilderness manna (Exod 16:4, dᵉḇar yôm bᵉyômô); the daily provision of the exile recalls the daily provision of the wilderness, both sustained by a sovereign outside Israel’s control. The book ends with the Davidic seed alive, receiving daily bread in a foreign land — a small, ambiguous ember of covenant hope.

12.1: Jehoash (Yəhôʾāš, YHWH-has-given) becomes king in Jehu's seventh year — the synchronism anchors him in the Jehu dynasty of Israel while his long forty-year reign in Jerusalem marks him as the most durable of the Athaliah-crisis survivors.
12.2: ʾăšer hôrāhû Yəhôyāḏāʿ hakkōhēn — 'because Jehoiada the priest instructed him.' The syntax makes Joash's righteousness contingent (kol-yəmê ʾăšer hôrāhû, 'all his days, to the extent that Jehoiada instructed him'); where the mentorship ends, the good ends with it (cf. vv20-21 — apostasy and assassination after Jehoiada's death in 2 Chr 24:17-22).
12.3: habbāmôt lōʾ-sārû — 'the high places were not removed.' The bāmôt qualification recurs in every otherwise-positive regnal evaluation in Kings; it marks the limit of the Josianic standard these pre-Josiah kings cannot reach and signals that the cult is still partially decentralized despite Jerusalem's central sanctuary.
12.4: Three categories of temple income: (1) kesef ʿōbēr — 'money from those who pass over,' the half-shekel census/poll tax of Exod 30:12-16; (2) kesef nepeš ʿerěkô — 'money of the soul of one's valuation,' the Levitical redemption assessments of Lev 27; (3) kesef yāḇîʾ ʾîš ʿal libbô — 'money any man brings on his heart,' i.e., freewill offerings. The classification shows Joash understood the three legally distinct funding streams and intended a systematic collection.
12.5: yəḥazzəqû ʾeṯ-beḏeq habbayiṯ — 'let them repair the breaches of the house.' beḏeq (breach, crack) is the structural term for damage to load-bearing walls; the priests are to collect the money and disburse it to the craftsmen who repair the actual stone and timber.
12.6: By the twenty-third year of Joash the priests had still not repaired the temple breaches — the system of v5 failed, either from priestly diversion of funds or administrative neglect. The failure prompts Joash's reform of the collection system in vv9-16.
12.7: Joash summons Jehoiada and the priests and prohibits them from receiving money from their acquaintances (mimmayyuddaʿăkhem) and from the repair obligation. The king does not punish for past failure but restructures the system going forward — a pragmatic rather than punitive response.
12.8: The priests agree to neither receive money nor repair the temple — they yield the fiscal responsibility back to the crown. This transfer of temple finances from priestly to royal oversight is a significant administrative shift in the relationship between palace and sanctuary.
12.9: Jehoiada the priest bored a hole (wayyiqboʿ ḥōr) in the lid of the chest (ʾārôn) and set it beside the altar at the right of the entrance. The gatekeeping priests put all the money brought into YHWH's house into it — a transparent, auditable collection system replacing the priestly ad hoc arrangement.
12.10: When the chest was full, the king's secretary and the high priest would count the silver and wrap it in bags. The dual-oversight system (royal secretary + high priest) provides accountability — neither party handles money alone.
12.11: The weighed-out money was given directly to the overseers (pəqûḏîm, the appointed ones) of the work on YHWH's house, who disbursed to carpenters and builders, masons and stonecutters, timber-buyers and hewn-stone-buyers. The craftsmen received wages directly, bypassing priestly intermediaries.
12.12: All the skilled trades required for temple restoration are listed — carpenters, builders, masons, stonecutters — plus quarried stone (ʾaḇnê māḥṣāḇ) and timber for the structural repairs. The enumeration signals both the scope and the competence of the project.
12.13: A notable limitation: the silver was not used to make silver basins, snuffers, bowls, trumpets, or gold and silver vessels for YHWH's house. The repair project was strictly structural, not decorative — the priority was the building's fabric, not its furnishings.
12.14: The craftsmen used the money for repairs without any accounting to those who gave it, kî beʾĕmûnāh hêm ʿōśîm — 'because they worked in faithfulness/trustworthiness.' ʾĕmûnāh (faithfulness) is the noun from ʾāman, which underlies the liturgical ʾāmēn; the craftsmen's integrity made detailed auditing unnecessary.
12.15: The same note as v14 — no accounting required because of the workers' ʾĕmûnāh. The double mention underscores that trustworthy stewardship makes bureaucratic oversight secondary; the integrity of the workers validates the entire project.
12.16: Guilt offering (ʾāšām) and sin offering (ḥaṭṭāʾṯ) money went to the priests separately — it was not deposited in the chest. The text thus distinguishes the two funding streams: building funds go into the chest; sacrificial-system proceeds remain in priestly hands. The two budgets do not mingle.
12.17: Hazael attacks Gath and turns to attack Jerusalem. Joash takes the qoḏāšîm (consecrated/sacred things) dedicated by his ancestors Jehoshaphat, Joram, Ahaziah, and himself, plus all the gold in the temple treasury, and sends them to Hazael. The bitter irony: the king who repaired the temple's structure strips its consecrated wealth to buy off a foreign threat — the temple budget built up over three reigns is dispatched in one payment.
12.18: wayyēleḵ mēʿal Yərûšālem — 'and he withdrew from Jerusalem.' The tribute payment works: Hazael turns away. But the cost was the sacred dedications, the gold reserves, and presumably the goodwill built by the temple repair project. The story implies that material security purchased at the cost of the sacred is not security at all.
12.19: The remaining acts of Joash are in the royal chronicle. His reign ends not with a full regnal account but with the assassination record that follows — the end is shaped by the murder, not by the building project.
12.20: Joash's servants conspire against him and strike him at bêṯ millôʾ hayyôrēḏ Sillāʾ — the house of Millo, which goes down to Silla. The location is unknown but it belongs to the Millo-complex, the fill-work of the older City of David. The assassination terminates a forty-year reign by a conspiracy among his own court.
12.21: The assassins are named: Jozachar son of Shimeath and Jehozabad son of Shomer. Both names and maternal pedigrees are given — the text does not conceal the perpetrators. Joash is buried with his fathers in the City of David; his son Amaziah succeeds him.

13.1: Jehoahaz son of Jehu begins to reign over Israel in Samaria in the twenty-third year of Joash of Judah. The synchronism ties the two kingdoms together chronologically through the Jehu dynasty and the Joash interregnum.
13.2: wayyēleḵ bəḥaṭṭôʾṯ Yārābəʿām — 'he walked in the sins of Jeroboam.' The fixed formulaic indictment of every northern king since 1 Kgs 12:30; its repetition signals that Jeroboam's golden calves function as a permanent structural sin of the northern kingdom, not merely individual apostasies.
13.3: YHWH's anger burns against Israel and he gives them into the hand of Hazael king of Aram and Ben-hadad son of Hazael — the two successive rulers of the Aramean empire who dominated Israel through most of Jehu's dynasty. The punishment is YHWH's direct action through foreign military pressure.
13.4: wayyəḥal Yəhôʾāḥāz ʾeṯ-pənê YHWH — 'Jehoahaz implored/sought the face of YHWH.' ḥālāh pānîm (to make the face of YHWH) is an idiom for earnest petition; ḥālāh literally means to be weak or sick, idiomatically to approach in humility. YHWH listened because he saw the oppression of Israel by the king of Aram — divine mercy operates on compassion for human suffering, not exclusively on merit.
13.5: wayyitten YHWH lə yiśrāʾēl môšîaʿ — 'YHWH gave Israel a deliverer/savior.' môšîaʿ is the participle of yāšaʿ, the same root as Yeshua; the historical deliverer here is likely an Assyrian king (Adad-nirari III pressed Aram) rather than an Israelite figure — YHWH's salvation instruments can be pagan powers. Israel dwelt in its tents as before, i.e., returned to normality; yet the sin of Jeroboam continued (v6).
13.6: Despite YHWH's deliverance, Israel did not depart from the house of Jeroboam. The ʾăšērāh (Asherah pole) still stood in Samaria — a visible symbol of syncretism at the capital. Deliverance from oppression did not produce repentance; the structural cult persisted.
13.7: Jehoahaz's remaining military force is reduced to fifty horsemen, ten chariots, ten thousand foot soldiers — kî ʾibbaḏām melek ʾărām wayyəśimēm kəʿāpār lāḏuš, 'because the king of Aram destroyed them and made them like dust for threshing.' The image of threshed dust conveys near-total pulverization; Israel's military capacity is barely functional.
13.8: The rest of Jehoahaz's acts — 'his might' (gəḇûrāṯô) and all he did — are in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel. The standard archival reference; the irony is that the chapter documenting his military humiliation must appeal to a record that uses the term 'might.'
13.9: Jehoahaz dies and is buried in Samaria; his son Joash (Jehoash) reigns in his place. The brief dynastic succession notice moves quickly to the more theologically significant reign of Jehoash, who will have the encounter with the dying Elisha.
13.10: Jehoash son of Jehoahaz begins to reign over Israel in Samaria in the thirty-seventh year of Joash of Judah, reigning sixteen years. The synchronism with Joash of Judah (a different Joash) produces a confusing name-overlap that the narrative handles by keeping strict synchronistic tracking.
13.11: wayyaʿaś hārāʿ bəʿênê YHWH lōʾ-sār mikkol-ḥaṭṭôʾṯ Yārābəʿām — the standard negative formula; Jehoash of Israel follows Jeroboam's sins despite being the king who will be instructed by the dying Elisha and who will achieve military victories against Aram.
13.12: The rest of Jehoash's acts include his 'might' and his war with Amaziah of Judah — a proleptic reference to the conflict described in ch14. The archival notice also appears in full in 14:15, suggesting the compiler has integrated the Elisha deathbed episode between the two archival references to Jehoash.
13.13: Jehoash dies and is buried in Samaria with the kings of Israel; Jeroboam II sits on his throne. The dynasty continues, but the text is about to look back at the extraordinary deathbed scene with Elisha before moving to Jeroboam II's long, prosperous reign.
13.14: Elisha falls ill with the illness from which he will die — in contrast to Elijah's translation without death (2:11), Elisha dies of sickness. Joash king of Israel comes down to him and weeps over his face, crying ʾāḇî ʾāḇî rekeḇ yiśrāʾēl ûpārāšāyw — 'my father, my father, the chariots of Israel and its horsemen.' This is verbatim Elisha's cry for Elijah in 2:12; Joash uses the same words that Elisha used, applying the title of prophetic intercessor now to Elisha himself. The prophet was Israel's real military defense — more than chariots.
13.15: Elisha instructs Joash to take a bow and arrows; the king takes them. The instruction moves from mourning to symbolic action — the deathbed becomes a prophetic stage.
13.16: Elisha instructs Joash to put his hand on the bow; Elisha then lays his own hands on the king's hands — the prophet's hands covering the king's hands on the bow is a transfer of prophetic authority to royal military action, a laying-on-of-hands impartation for the coming campaign.
13.17: ḥēṣ-tešûʿāh laYHWH wəḥēṣ-tešûʿāh bə ʾărām — 'arrow of YHWH's salvation (tešûʿāh) and arrow of salvation against Aram.' tešûʿāh derives from yāšaʿ, the same root as Yeshua/Joshua — salvation-vocabulary infuses the military prophecy; Elisha declares total victory: Joash will strike Aram at Aphek until he has finished them.
13.18: Elisha tells Joash to strike the arrows on the ground; the king strikes three times and stops. Elisha is angry: ləhakkôṯ ḥāmêš ʾô-šēš pəʿāmîm — 'you should have struck five or six times, then you would have struck Aram until you finished it; now you will strike Aram only three times.' The king's half-committed response limits the scope of YHWH's victory; incomplete faith produces incomplete deliverance.
13.19: The three limited strikes translate into three limited military victories over Aram — the prophet's word binds the event; the king's action in the prophetic drama predetermined the outcome. The lesson is not that YHWH's power is limited but that the human receptacle limits what YHWH will pour into it.
13.20: Elisha dies and is buried. The notice is stark: wayyāmāṯ ʾĕlîšāʿ wayyiqbəruhû — 'and Elisha died and they buried him.' The contrast with Elijah is total: no chariot, no fire, no translation — just death and burial. Yet the death leads immediately to the postmortem miracle of v21.
13.21: wayyəhî hêm qōḇərîm ʾîš wəhinnēh rāʾû ʾeṯ-haggəḏûḏ wayyašlîḵû ʾeṯ-hāʾîš bəqeḇer ʾĕlîšāʿ wayyēleḵ wayyiggaʿ hāʾîš bəʿaṣmôṯ ʾĕlîšāʿ wayyəḥî wayyāqom ʿal-ragəlāyw — the syntax tumbles with the urgency of the event: they were burying a man, saw raiders, threw the body into Elisha's tomb; the man touched Elisha's bones, wayyəḥî wayyāqom — 'he lived and he stood up on his feet.' The two verbs ḥāyāh (live) and qûm (rise) are resurrection vocabulary; the dead bones of YHWH's prophet still carry his prophetic charge. This is the last Elisha story in Kings.
13.22: Hazael king of Aram oppressed Israel all the days of Jehoahaz — a retrospective summary that frames the entire Jehoahaz-to-Jehoash period under Aramean military pressure. The oppression is the context against which YHWH's compassion and the Elisha prophecies operate.
13.23: wayyəḥunnêm wayyeraḥămêm wayyipen ʾălêhem lǝmaʿan bərîṯô ʾeṯ-ʾaḇrāhām yiṣḥāq wəyaʿăqōḇ — 'YHWH was gracious to them, had compassion on them, and turned to them for the sake of his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.' Three divine verbs: ḥānan (grace), riḥam (compassion/mercy), pānāh (turn/face). YHWH's preservation of Israel is explicitly covenantal — not because Israel deserves it but because YHWH bound himself to Abraham; the Abrahamic covenant underwrites discipline-era Israel's survival.
13.24: Hazael king of Aram dies; Ben-hadad his son reigns in his place. The death of Israel's greatest oppressor opens the possibility for the three Elisha-prophesied victories Jehoash will now achieve against the less powerful Ben-hadad III.
13.25: Jehoash defeats Ben-hadad three times — exactly the three times prophesied by Elisha's arrow-striking ritual (v18-19). wayyāšeḇ ʾeṯ-ʿārê yiśrāʾēl — 'he restored the cities of Israel,' specifically the cities that Hazael had taken from Jehoahaz. Prophetic word becomes historical event precisely as the prophetic act signified; the limitation was the king's three strikes, not YHWH's capacity.

14.1: Amaziah son of Joash begins to reign as king of Judah in the second year of Jehoash son of Jehoahaz of Israel. The synchronism links the Judahite Joash's son to the Israelite Jehoash's second year — a dense chronological web.
14.2: Amaziah is twenty-five at accession and reigns twenty-nine years. His mother Jehoaddan is from Jerusalem — a purely Judahite lineage, unlike some queens with foreign connections. The maternal data grounds the king in the Jerusalem community.
14.3: wayyaʿaś hayyāšār bəʿênê YHWH ʾaḵ lōʾ kəḏāwiḏ ʾāḇîw kəkōl ʾăšer-ʿāśāh Yôʾāš ʾāḇîw ʿāśāh — 'he did right in YHWH's eyes, but not like David his father; according to all that Joash his father had done, he did.' The double qualification: he was better than some but the Davidic standard is still the ceiling he does not reach. His father Joash is the reference-point for his practice, not the ideal David.
14.4: The bāmôt formula again: high places not removed; the people still sacrifice and burn incense at the high places. Amaziah's otherwise-positive reign is limited by the decentralized cult that only Josiah will finally address.
14.5: Once Amaziah's kingdom was firmly in hand (kahăzaqāh hamamlākāh bəyāḏô), he struck down the servants who had killed his father the king. Political consolidation precedes the act of justice — the sequence is deliberate; Amaziah waits until his position is secure before acting against the assassins.
14.6: wəʾeṯ-bənê hammaккîm lōʾ hēmîṯ kikāṯûḇ bəsēper ṯôraṯ-môšeh ʾăšer-ṣiwwāh YHWH lēʾmōr lōʾ-yûmtû ʾāḇôṯ ʿal-bānîm ûḇānîm lōʾ-yûmtû ʿal-ʾāḇôṯ kî ʾim-ʾîš bəḥeṭʾô yûmāṯ — 'but the sons of the killers he did not put to death, as written in the Book of the Law of Moses, which YHWH commanded: fathers shall not be put to death for sons, nor sons for fathers; each for his own sin shall he be put to death' (Deut 24:16). This is one of the most explicit Deuteronomic citations in Kings, naming sēper ṯôraṯ-môšeh as the canonical authority. Amaziah applies individual-guilt doctrine against collective-family punishment — a legal principle that resists the ancient Near Eastern norm of clan accountability.
14.7: Amaziah defeats ten thousand Edomites in the Valley of Salt (gê-hammelaḥ) and takes Sela (haSSəlaʿ = the rock/cliff), renaming it Joktheel (yoqṯəʾēl). The Valley of Salt links to David's victories in the same locale (2 Sam 8:13); Amaziah's Edom campaign is a minor Davidic echo, though without the significance of the original.
14.8: Amaziah sends envoys to Jehoash of Israel: lēḵāh niṯrāʾeh pānîm — 'come, let us look each other in the face,' i.e., meet in battle. The challenge is voluntary military aggression after the Edomite victory — success leading to overreach, a pattern the thistle-cedar parable in v9 directly names.
14.9: Jehoash sends back the thistle-cedar parable: a thistle in Lebanon sent to a cedar of Lebanon saying 'give your daughter to my son as wife'; then a wild beast passed by and trampled the thistle. The parable maps as: Amaziah = thistle, Jehoash = cedar; the wild beast = military reality that will crush a small king who overestimates himself. The imagery is from the genre of royal beast-fable; Jehoash deploys it as a dismissal and a warning.
14.10: hiḵêṯā ʾeṯ ʾĕḏôm ûnsāʾăḵā libbεḵā — 'you have defeated Edom and your heart has lifted you up.' nāśāʾ lēḇāḇ = pride/arrogance, the standard Hebrew idiom for hubris; military success has inflated Amaziah's self-assessment. Jehoash's advice: stay home and glory in your victory — why court disaster?
14.11: Amaziah does not listen (lōʾ šāmaʿ). The refusal to heed prophetic-parabolic warning is the pivot of the narrative; what follows is judgment on the refusal. Jehoash and Amaziah face each other at Beth-shemesh in Judah — the battle is in Judahite territory, an invasion of the south.
14.12: Judah is defeated before Israel; every man flees to his home. wayyinnāgep Yəhûḏāh — Judah is struck/routed. The defeat is total and humiliating; a Judahite king whose overconfidence led him to invade Israel is beaten on his own soil.
14.13: Jehoash captures Amaziah at Beth-shemesh and brings him to Jerusalem. He then tears down the Jerusalem wall from the Gate of Ephraim to the Corner Gate — four hundred cubits of the city wall — breaching the city's defenses. The physical breach enacts the humiliation; Amaziah's overconfidence ends in his capital's wall destroyed.
14.14: Jehoash takes all the gold and silver and all the vessels found in YHWH's house and the palace, plus hostages, and returns to Samaria. The looting parallels Shishak's plunder under Rehoboam (1 Kgs 14:25-26) and Hazael's extortion under Joash (12:18) — the temple and palace assets circulate between kingdoms as military spoils.
14.15: The rest of Jehoash's acts — his might (gəḇûrāṯô) and his war with Amaziah — are in the chronicles of the kings of Israel. This is the second archival notice for Jehoash (the first in 13:12), forming brackets around the Elisha deathbed and the Amaziah battle sections.
14.16: Jehoash dies and is buried in Samaria with the kings of Israel; Jeroboam II his son reigns in his place. The short notice launches the most economically and militarily successful reign in Israel's history.
14.17: Amaziah outlives Jehoash by fifteen years — he reigns twenty-nine years total, the last fifteen as a defeated, partially humiliated king. The text notes his survival after Jehoash's death without suggesting rehabilitation; the defeat defined the rest of his reign.
14.18: The rest of Amaziah's acts are in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah — the archival reference for Judah's kings, parallel to Israel's chronicle.
14.19: A conspiracy against Amaziah arises in Jerusalem; he flees to Lachish (lāḵîš), the fortified Judahite city in the Shephelah. They send after him to Lachish and kill him there. The king who began by lawfully punishing his father's assassins ends by being assassinated himself — an ironic structural closure on the Joash-Amaziah regnal pair.
14.20: They bring his body on horses and bury him with his fathers in Jerusalem in the City of David. The burial honors the Davidic dynastic convention; despite assassination, he receives royal burial.
14.21: All the people of Judah (ʿam Yəhûḏāh) take Azariah (Uzziah), sixteen years old, and make him king in place of his father Amaziah. The people's role in the accession is unusual — normally succession is automatic; the people's active choice suggests a period of instability after the assassination.
14.22: Azariah built Elath (ʾêlaṯ) and restored it to Judah — probably the Red Sea port (Ezion-geber area) that gives access to trade routes; this brief note of building and restoration is one of the more positive administrative achievements attributed to any Judahite king in this period.
14.23: Jeroboam II son of Jehoash begins to reign over Israel in Samaria in the fifteenth year of Amaziah of Judah. His forty-one year reign (the longest of any northern king) makes him the most durable ruler of the Jehu dynasty and of the northern kingdom altogether.
14.24: wayyaʿaś hārāʿ bəʿênê YHWH lōʾ-sār mikkol-ḥaṭṭôʾṯ Yārābəʿām — the standard northern-kingdom indictment despite Jeroboam II's extraordinary political and military success. The Deuteronomic historian evaluates by covenant faithfulness, not by political achievement; the most successful northern king still receives the lowest theological grade.
14.25: hûʾ hēšîḇ ʾeṯ-gəḇûl yiśrāʾēl mimmābôʾ ḥămaṯ ʿaḏ-yām hāʿărāḇāh kidbar YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl ʾăšer dibber bəyaḏ-ʿaḇḏô Yônāh ḇen-ʾĂmiṯṯay hannāḇîʾ — 'he restored the border of Israel from Lebo-hamath to the Sea of the Arabah, according to the word of YHWH the God of Israel, which he spoke by his servant Jonah son of Amittai, the prophet.' This is the only mention of Jonah ben Amittai in Kings; he is identified as a prophet who proclaimed Israel's territorial restoration — a very different commission from the Nineveh mission of the book named for him. ʿeḇeḏ YHWH (servant of YHWH) here applied to Jonah is the same designation as the Isaianic Servant Songs; Jonah is simultaneously a historical prophet and an ironic figure whose own book records his reluctance to deliver YHWH's word.
14.26: ʾêyn ʿōṣēr wəʾêyn ʿāzēr lə yiśrāʾēl — 'there was no one restraining and no one helping for Israel'; the nation was in its most desperate condition. YHWH saw Israel's exceedingly bitter affliction (mar məʾōḏ) and that there was none — literally, no deliverer. The divine seeing and responding to communal desperate suffering underlies the restoration through Jeroboam II.
14.27: wəlōʾ-ḏibber YHWH lǝmāḥôṯ ʾeṯ-šēm yiśrāʾēl mittaḥaṯ haššāmayim — 'and YHWH had not spoken to blot out the name of Israel from under heaven.' The Deuteronomic covenant includes total covenant dissolution as the ultimate sanction; YHWH's restraint from enacting this — despite continued Jeroboam-pattern sins — is attributed to his compassion (v26). YHWH saves Israel through Jeroboam II not because of the king's righteousness but because of his own prior commitment not to blot out the name.
14.28: The rest of Jeroboam II's acts — his might (gəḇûrāṯô), his wars, his restoration of Damascus and Hamath to Judah (reading with LXX) — are in the chronicles of the kings of Israel. The extraordinary scope of his campaigns is noted and then referenced to the archive; the historian is more interested in covenant evaluation than in military geography.
14.29: Jeroboam II dies and is buried in Samaria with the kings of Israel; his son Zechariah reigns in his place. The single succession line that ends four generations later in Zechariah's assassination (2 Kgs 15:10) closes the Jehu dynasty — the longest-ruling northern dynasty ends with the dynasty's predicted limit (2 Kgs 10:30: four generations on the throne).

6.1: bĕnê hannĕbîʾîm — ‘sons of the prophets.’ The word bēn marks discipleship, not biological relationship: these are members of a prophetic guild attached to Elisha. The plural construct bĕnê identifies a community of trainees, paralleling the guild communities at Bethel and Gilgal (2 Kgs 2:3, 4:38). The phrase places 2 Kings 6 firmly within the institutional prophetic tradition that runs from Samuel through Elijah to Elisha.
6.2: The sons of the prophets request to build a new meeting-place at the Jordan. nillāqaḥ (cohortative, ‘let us take’) expresses collective deliberation and initiative. The detail that the place where they live ‘is too cramped (ṣar) for us’ uses the same root word (ṣārar) used for the constraint of Egypt; the community is outgrowing its space, the opposite of the covenant curse of shrinkage.
6.3: One of the sons requests Elisha to come with them: hôʾēl nā ûlēk ʾet-ʿăḇādêkā — ‘please be willing to go with your servants.’ hôʾîl is a causative stem of yāʾal, meaning to be willing, to consent, to undertake. Elisha’s one-word answer — ʾēlēk, ‘I will go’ — mirrors the brevity of divine assent elsewhere (cf. the divine lēkā to Moses). The man of God follows his community into ordinary labor.
6.4: Simple narrative sequence using the waw-consecutive (wayyēlek... wayĕhî... wayyipplû): they went, they came, they felled. The rapid perfective narration (wayyiqṭōl forms) moves the story forward without elaboration. The Jordan is the scene of multiple Elisha miracles; the crossing point of covenant entry (Josh 3) becomes the site of the prophetic community’s building project.
6.5: ʾăhāh ʾăḏōnî — an interjection of distress: ‘Oh, my master! It was borrowed.’ The key word is šāʾûl (borrowed, from šāʾal): the axe head was a loan. This is not a complaint about the loss alone but a legal anxiety — a borrowed tool that is lost creates an obligation of restitution (Exod 22:14). The exclamation foregrounds the narrator’s interest in the socioeconomic vulnerability of the prophetic community.
6.6: Elisha asks, ʾānāh nāpal — ‘where did it fall?’ Then he cuts a stick (ʿēṣ) and throws it at the place — and the iron floats. The verb wayyāṣēp (it floated) is from ṣûp, which means to float or swim; the same root used for the basket in Exod 2:3 (Moses’s ark). The miracle is accomplished through the cast stick, an ordinary material whose use is not explained — the instrument is instrumental, the power is divine.
6.7: hārîm lāk — ‘pick it up for yourself.’ The imperative hārîm (lift/raise) with lāk (for yourself, reflexive) assigns the man both the instruction and the benefit. The miracle is not spectacular display but practical restoration — the borrowed iron is returned, the debt is cleared. The economy of grace operates through simple command and simple obedience.
6.8: melek ʾărām nōḥēm milḥāmāh — ‘the king of Aram was making war.’ The participle nōḥēm (deliberating, planning) from yāʿaṣ — to counsel, to plan. The king ‘counsels with his officers’ (nîʿāṣ ʾel-ʿăḇādāyw) in tactical secrecy, saying ‘at such-and-such a place (māqôm) shall be my camp.’ The king’s secret military strategy is the foil for the prophet who knows all secrets.
6.9: šālaḥ ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm — ‘the man of God sent word.’ šālaḥ (sent) with no object for the message marks urgency. His warning, hišāmĕr mēʿăḇōr hammāqôm hazzeh — ‘take care not to pass that place’ — uses hišāmēr (guard yourself, beware) with min- (from), the standard warning construction. The information revealed is not from military intelligence but from divine revelation — the narration has the king of Israel discover that Elisha’s intelligence is prophetic, not human (v12).
6.10: The king of Israel sent to the places the man of God warned about and ‘was on his guard there’ — nišmar šām (guarded himself there). The Hebrew is terse; the king’s response is immediate and effective. The narrator notes this happened ‘more than once or twice’ (lōʾ ʾaḥat wĕlōʾ šĕtāyim, ‘not once and not twice’) — a Hebrew idiom for repeated occurrences, underscoring the pattern.
6.11: wayyiṣṭarab lēb melek ʾărām — ‘the heart of the king of Aram was troubled/stormy.’ The verb sāʿar in Niphal means to be stormy, disturbed, agitated — used of a stormy sea. The lēb (heart) as seat of understanding and decision is troubled because the confidentiality of royal counsel has been breached. The king suspects a traitor among his officers: mî mišelānû ʾel-melek yiśrāʾēl — ‘which of us is for the king of Israel?’
6.12: The officer’s answer names Elisha: lōʾ ʾăḏōnî hammelek kî-ʾĕlîšāʿ hannāḇîʾ — ‘none, my lord the king, but Elisha the prophet.’ The subsequent description — yagîd lĕmelek yiśrāʾēl ʾet-haddĕḇārîm ʾăšer tĕdabbēr bĕḥadar miškābĕkā — ‘he tells the king of Israel the words you speak in your bedroom.’ The phrase bĕḥadar miškābĕkā (in your bedroom chamber) echoes Eccl 10:20 and emphasizes the most private possible speech. The prophet’s knowledge is portrayed as total omniscience, an extension of YHWH’s knowing all things (Ps 139:4).
6.13: The king commands, lĕkû rĕʾû ʾêkōh hûʾ — ‘go, see where he is.’ The imperative plural and the king’s intent — wĕʾešlaḥ wĕʾeqqāḥeyhû, ‘I will send and seize him’ — use lāqaḥ (take/seize) for arrest. The response: hinnēh bĕḏōṯān, ‘behold, he is in Dothan.’ Dothan appears in Gen 37:17 as the place where Joseph was sold by his brothers — the site carries connotations of betrayal and divine rescue.
6.14: The king sent a great army — sûs wārekeb wĕḥayil kāḇēḏ — ‘horses and chariots and a heavy force.’ The adjective kāḇēḏ (heavy) is used of the Egyptians’ heavy/hard heart at the Exodus and of armies that overwhelm. They arrived lāylāh (at night) and surrounded the city — standard ANE siege preparation under cover of darkness.
6.15: Elisha’s servant (mĕšārēt, attendant/minister — the same word used of Joshua’s service to Moses, Exod 24:13) rises early and goes out. wayyiśśāʾ ʾet-ʿênāyw wayyarʾ — ‘and he lifted his eyes and saw.’ The compound idiom nāśāʾ ʿênayim (lift the eyes) introduces visually significant moments throughout the OT (Gen 13:14; Josh 5:13). His cry to Elisha: ʾăḥāh ʾăḏōnî mah naʿăśeh, ‘alas, my master, what shall we do?’
6.16: lōʾ-tîrāʾ kî rabbîm ʾăšer ʾittānû mēʾăšer ʾittām — ‘Do not be afraid, for those who are with us are more than those who are with them.’ The chiasmic comparison (rabbîm ʾăšer ʾittānû vs. ʾăšer ʾittām) mirrors the formula of Num 14:9 and Josh 1:9. The key word is rāb (many, great): the unseen army is numerically greater, though invisible. This is the Hebrew equivalent of Rom 8:31: ‘if God is for us, who can be against us?’ — the numerical calculus of faith.
6.17: Elisha’s prayer: YHWH pĕqaḥ-nā ʾet-ʿênāyw wĕyirʾeh — ‘LORD, please open his eyes that he may see.’ The verb pāqaḥ (open — specifically of eyes) is used in Gen 3:5 (the serpent’s promise: ‘your eyes will be opened’) and Gen 21:19 (Hagar’s eyes opened to see the well). Divine eye-opening is always a gift of sight beyond ordinary seeing. The servant sees sûsîm wĕrekeb ʾēš — ‘horses and chariots of fire.’ The phrase exactly parallels 2:11 (Elijah’s ascent) and brackets the Elisha narrative with fire-chariots at beginning and end of the prophetic succession.
6.18: Elisha prays a second time: hak nā ʾet-haggôy-hazzeh bassanwĕrîm — ‘please strike this people with blindness/dazzlement.’ The word sanwĕrîm is a hapax legomenon (appears only here and Gen 19:11, where the angels strike the Sodomites with the same word). It likely derives from a root related to dazzling brightness, not total darkness — a supernatural disorientation of sight. The Arameans are not blinded in the medical sense (they can still walk, following Elisha); they are divinely confused about where they are and who they are following.
6.19: Elisha’s deception of the disoriented Arameans — lōʾ zeh hadderek wĕlōʾ zōʾt hāʿîr — ‘This is not the way and this is not the city.’ He leads them to Samaria, the capital. The verb ʾōlîk ʾetkem (I will lead you) uses the Hiphil of hālak — causative leading, typically used of God leading his people (the Exodus). Elisha is re-enacting the divine leading of the desert, but to the center of Israel’s power rather than the promised land.
6.20: On arrival in Samaria, Elisha prays a third time: YHWH pĕqaḥ ʾet-ʿênê ʾēlleh wĕyirʾû — ‘LORD, open the eyes of these men that they may see.’ The same verb pāqaḥ used in v17 creates a double eye-opening: the servant’s eyes opened to see the divine army; the Arameans’ eyes opened to see themselves surrounded. The verse contains an ironic inclusio between v17 and v20: both use ‘open his/their eyes’ to reveal reality, but one revelation is salvation and the other is captivity.
6.21: The king of Israel’s question: hakkōh ʾakkeh ʾăbî hakkōh ʾakkeh — ‘Shall I strike them, my father? Shall I strike them?’ The doubled question with hakkōh (thus/in this manner) expresses eagerness. The address ʾāḇî (my father) to Elisha is the same address Elisha used for Elijah (2:12); it marks the prophet as a paternal authority over the king. The repetition of the question intensifies both the eagerness and the waiting for prophetic authorization.
6.22: Elisha’s prohibition: lōʾ takkeh — ‘You shall not strike them.’ His reasoning uses a rhetorical question: ʾăšer šāḇîtā bĕḥarbekā ûbĕqaštĕkā, ‘those you have captured with your sword and your bow.’ The point is that these men were not captured by Israel’s military force at all — God delivered them. Elisha then commands: śîm lāhem leḥem wāmayim wĕyōʾkĕlû wĕyišĕtû, ‘set bread and water before them that they may eat and drink.’ The feeding of an enemy army by prophetic command has no parallel in the OT.
6.23: wayyikrōt lāhem kĕrāh gĕḏôlāh — ‘he prepared a great feast for them.’ kārāh (to prepare a feast) is used of festive meals in Gen 31:54 and Prov 9:2. The great feast given to enemies recalls the covenant meal (cf. Exod 24:11, the elders eating before God on Sinai). After eating and drinking, wayyĕšallĕḥēm (he sent them away) — the Piel of šālaḥ, the same verb used of releasing slaves and prisoners. The Aramean incursions then ceased: lōʾ-yāsĕpû ʿôḏ — ‘they came no more.’
6.24: wayyĕhî ʾaḥărê-kēn — ‘after this.’ The narrator’s transitional formula signals a major scene shift: the Aramean king Ben-hadad (ben-hadad melek ʾărām) musters his entire army (ʾet-kol-maḥănēhû) and goes up to besiege Samaria. The verb wayyiṣōr (laid siege) from ṣûr marks prolonged military pressure. This is the systematic siege that will produce the famine of vv25-33.
6.25: wayhî rāʿāḇ gāḏôl bĕšōmĕrôn — ‘there was a great famine in Samaria.’ The severity is measured by market prices: ḥōmĕr rōʾš ḥamôr bĕŠmōnîm kesep, ‘a donkey’s head for eighty shekels.’ The donkey is a non-kosher animal (Lev 11:4-7), its head the least valuable part. Normal price of a lamb at Passover: approximately 5 shekels. The price escalation represents a factor of over 200: the covenant economy has collapsed entirely, as Deut 28:52-57 predicted for a siege.
6.26: wayyiṣʿaq ʾēlāyw ʾiššāh — ‘a woman cried out to him.’ The verb ṣāʿaq (cry out, cry for help) is used throughout the OT for the cry of the oppressed (Exod 2:23; Judg 3:15). The king is addressed as ʾăḏōnî hammelek (my lord the king), the standard petition formula — the woman is using the formal channels of royal justice.
6.27: The king’s response uses two Hebrew nouns in a rhetorical pair: min-haggōren ʾô min-hayyāqeb — ‘from the threshing floor or from the winepress?’ These are the two sources of grain and wine — but the king’s bitter question means: if YHWH won’t help you, what source of provision remains? The king has no grain from the floor and no wine from the press during a siege. His question is a confession of helplessness dressed as a rhetorical question.
6.28: The king asks what is wrong; the woman’s account is a nightmare: her neighbor said tĕnî ʾet-binkê wĕnōklennû hayyôm — ‘give your son so we can eat him today.’ The verb ʾākal (eat) and the causative nātan (give) in this context carry the full weight of Deut 28:53: ‘you shall eat the offspring of your own body, the flesh of your sons and daughters.’ The covenant curse is not a hyperbole; it is literally enacted.
6.29: The woman’s account continues: wattōʾmer ûḇannāh bĕneyhā — ‘and on the next day she hid her son.’ The verb ṣāpan (hide, conceal) used here for concealing a child recalls Exod 2:2-3 (the mother who hid Moses for three months). The reversal is devastating: in Exodus, a mother concealed a child to save him from slaughter; here, a mother conceals a child from a woman who wants to eat him. The Deuteronomic curse has inverted the Exodus rescue.
6.30: When the king heard the woman’s words, wayyiqraʿ ʾet-bĕḡādāyw — ‘he tore his clothes.’ The royal mourning gesture (cf. 2 Sam 3:31) expresses anguish and helplessness. The narrator adds that he was passing along the wall — his torn garments were visible to the people below (wĕhāʿām hišqîp wĕhinnēh hassaq, ‘and the people looked, and behold, sackcloth inside’). The king’s private mourning becomes a public spectacle.
6.31: The king’s oath against Elisha: kōh-yaʿăśeh lî ʾĕlōhîm wĕkōh yôsîp ʾim-yaʿămōd rōʾš ʾĕlîšāʿ — ‘may God deal with me... if the head of Elisha remains on him today.’ The self-imprecatory formula (kōh yaʿăśeh... wĕkōh yôsîp, ‘so may he do and so may he add’) is the standard Hebrew oath formula (cf. 1 Sam 14:44; Ruth 1:17). The blame directed at Elisha (rather than at YHWH) reflects a pattern of scapegoating the prophet for divine judgment.
6.32: Elisha is sitting with the elders (zĕqēnîm). He sees the messenger coming: rĕʾîtem kî-šālaḥ ben-hammĕraṣṣēaḥ hazzeh — ‘see how this son of a murderer has sent.’ The phrase ben-hammĕraṣṣēaḥ (son of a murderer) is a Hebrew idiom: bĕn + noun = one characterized by that quality (cf. bĕn-ḥayil, ‘son of valor’ = valiant man). Elisha’s preemptive announcement of the messenger’s arrival demonstrates prophetic foreknowledge a second time in the chapter.
6.33: The messenger arrives and the king’s despairing words: hinnēh zōʾt hārāʿāh mēʾēt YHWH mah-ʾôḥil lĕYHWH ʿôḏ — ‘this disaster is from YHWH — what further hope is there from YHWH?’ The word rāʿāh (disaster, evil) is the same word used throughout for moral evil and for catastrophe; here it describes the siege and famine as YHWH’s act. The king’s mah-ʾôḥil (what hope?) is a rare verb form (yāḥal, to wait/hope) — the despairing acknowledgment that YHWH is the source of the crisis and the only possible source of relief.

7.1: Elisha’s oracle begins with šimʿû dĕbar-YHWH — ‘hear the word of YHWH’ — the standard call to prophetic attention. The prediction: kĕsāʾāh sōlet bĕšeqel ûsātayim śĕʿōrîm bĕšeqel — ‘a seah of fine flour for a shekel, two seahs of barley for a shekel.’ This is a dramatic price reversal from 6:25 (where a donkey’s head costs 80 shekels). The timing: kāʿēt māḥār, ‘about this time tomorrow.’ The preciseness of the timing is part of the prophecy’s verification function.
7.2: The officer’s objection: wĕhinnēh ʾĕlōhîm ʿōśeh ʾărubbôt baššāmāyim hăyihyeh haddāḇār hazzeh — ‘even if YHWH were to make windows in heaven, could this thing happen?’ The phrase ʾărubbôt baššāmāyim (windows in heaven) is taken directly from Gen 7:11 — the flood narrative where ‘the windows of heaven were opened.’ The officer’s sarcasm uses the flood idiom to mock the possibility of supernatural provision. Elisha’s response: hinnĕkā rōʾeh bĕʿênêkā ûmišām lōʾ tōʾkēl, ‘you shall see it with your own eyes but not eat of it.’
7.3: The lepers at the gate: ʾarbaʿāh ʾănāšîm hāyû mĕṣōrāʿîm — ‘four men with skin disease.’ The word mĕṣōrāʿîm (from ṣāraʿ) denotes a severe skin condition requiring social exclusion (Lev 13-14); their position at the gate entrance places them at the margin between the city and the outside — they are excluded from the community. Their logic is expressed in a binary: they can neither enter the city (famine within) nor stay at the gate (starvation there too).
7.4: The lepers’ reasoning uses the imperfect tense for both contingencies: ʾim-ʾāmarnû nāḇôʾ hāʿîr wĕhārāʿāb bāʿîr ûmātnû šām — ‘If we say, let us enter the city, the famine is in the city and we will die there.’ The structure is a classic Hebrew conditional: ʾim + imperfect... wĕ-perfect. The conclusion: wĕnābôʾ ʾel-maḥănēh ʾărām ʾim-yĕḥayûnû niḥyeh, ‘if they spare us alive, we shall live; if they kill us, we shall die.’ This double conditional (ʾim-yĕḥayûnû / ʾim-yĕmîtûnû) is the syntax of a calculated gamble on mercy.
7.5: wayyāqûmû bannešep lābōʾ ʾel-maḥănēh ʾărām — ‘they rose at twilight to go to the Aramean camp.’ Nešep means twilight, the ambiguous light between day and night. The temporal detail heightens suspense. They arrive at the edge of the camp (qĕṣēh maḥănēh ʾărām) — and wĕhinnēh ʾên-šām ʾîš, ‘and behold, there was no one there.’ The hinnēh particle introduces a dramatic reversal discovery.
7.6: The divine action: waʾdōnāy hišmîaʿ ʾet-maḥănēh ʾărām qôl rekeb — ‘the Lord had made the Aramean camp hear the sound of chariots.’ The verb hišmîaʿ (Hiphil of šāmaʿ, to hear) means to cause to hear — God made them hear a sound that was not physically present. The sounds catalogued: chariots (rekeb), horses (sûs), a great army (ḥayil gāḏôl). The Arameans interpreted it as a coalition of Egypt and the Hittite kings (Israel hired them, they said) — a false reading of a true divine signal.
7.7: The rout of the Arameans: wayyāqûmû wayyanûsû bannešep — ‘they rose and fled at twilight.’ The same word nešep as v5 — the lepers came at twilight; the Arameans fled at twilight. The temporal irony binds the two groups: the marginalized move toward hope while the powerful move in terror. They abandoned their camp fully equipped: ʾohŏlêhem wĕsûsêhem waḥămōrêhem — tents, horses, donkeys — and wayyibrĕḥû ʾel-napšām, ‘they fled for their lives.’ Nepeš (life, soul) here means their physical survival.
7.8: The lepers’ discovery: wayyābōʾû hamĕṣōrāʿîm hāʾēlleh ʾel-qĕṣēh hammahăneh — ‘these lepers came to the edge of the camp.’ They ‘came to’ the edge, ate, drank, carried off silver, gold, and clothing. The verb wayyiśśĕʾû (they carried off) followed by the accumulation of wealth: kesep wĕzāhāḇ ûbĕḡāḏîm. Their immediate response is private hoarding — they carry it and hide it (wayyiṭmĕnû, from ṭāman, to bury/conceal) before returning for more.
7.9: wayyōʾmĕrû ʾîš ʾel-rēʿēhû lōʾ-kēn ʾănaḥnû ʿōśîm — ‘They said to one another, We are not doing right.’ The word kēn (right, thus) in the construction lōʾ-kēn means ‘not rightly.’ The key phrase: yôm-bĕśôrāh hûʾ — ‘this is a day of good news.’ Bĕśôrāh is the Hebrew equivalent of euangelion — the announcement of something that changes everything. The same root (Piel of bāśar) is used in Isa 52:7 (‘how beautiful are the feet of him who brings good news’). The lepers recognize their obligation to proclaim, not hoard, the discovery.
7.10: The lepers call to the city gatekeepers — wayyiqrĕʾû ʾel-šōʿar hāʿîr — and report: bānû ʾel-maḥănēh ʾărām wĕhinnēh ʾên-šām ʾîš wĕqôl ʾāḏām, ‘we came to the Aramean camp and behold, there was no one there, and no sound of a human voice.’ The repeated hinnēh (behold) marks the surprise. The compound negative (ʾên-šām ʾîš + wĕqôl ʾāḏām) emphasizes total abandonment — both persons and sounds are absent.
7.11: The gatekeepers call out; the report reaches the palace: wayyuggad lĕbêt hammelek pĕnîmāh — ‘it was reported to the king’s house inside.’ The adverb pĕnîmāh (inside, inward) and the passive wayyuggad (it was told) shift the verb to the divine passive — news reaches the center of power by unseen agents.
7.12: The king rises at night — the same ironic timing as the Aramean flight (twilight/night). His suspicion: ʾaggîḏāh-nā lākem ʾēt ʾăšer-ʿāśû-lānû ʾărām — ‘let me tell you what the Arameans have done.’ He suspects a trap: yōdĕʿîm hēmmāh kî-rĕʿēbîm ʾănaḥnû, ‘they know we are hungry.’ His military logic is sound — but his reading of the situation is completely wrong. The narrative irony is that the king’s elaborate suspicion keeps him from acting on genuine deliverance.
7.13: The king’s servant suggests a modest reconnaissance: wĕyišlĕḥû wĕnirʾeh — ‘let us send and see.’ The argument: the horses that remain are ‘like all the multitude of Israel that will be destroyed’ (kĕkol-hāmôn yiśrāʾēl ʾăšer niš’ārû-ḇām). The syntax implies they are expendable — even if it’s a trap, the loss of five horses equals only the guaranteed loss of dying in the city. This is the logic of the lepers in v4, applied by the king’s servants: marginal losses are acceptable when total loss is the alternative.
7.14: Two chariot teams are sent (šĕnê rikbê sûsîm). The king commands: lĕkû ûrĕʾû — ‘go and see.’ The brief direct command uses the plural imperative without elaboration — the mission is simple. The king adds: wĕšallĕḥēm ʾaḥărê maḥănēh ʾărām, ‘send them after the Aramean camp.’ The verb šālaḥ (send forth) with ʾaḥărê (after) marks pursuit — the king thinks this might be a rearguard trap.
7.15: The scouts follow to the Jordan: wayyēlĕkû ʾaḥărêhem ʿaḏ-hayyardēn. The road was littered with garments and equipment the Arameans threw off in their panic — kol-hadderek mĕlēʾāh bĕḡāḏîm ûkēlîm. The word kēlîm (vessels, equipment) indicates military gear abandoned in flight. The scouts return and report. The abandoned equipment confirms the divine rout — the Arameans stripped themselves to flee faster, a sign of total panic.
7.16: wayyēṣēʾ hāʿām wayyābōzẑû ʾet-maḥănēh ʾărām — ‘the people went out and plundered the Aramean camp.’ The verb bāzaz (to plunder, spoil) is used of the Israelites plundering Egypt at the Exodus (Exod 12:36). The echo is intentional: YHWH’s deliverance of Israel from a siege produces the same plundering of the enemy’s goods. The price of flour immediately fulfills Elisha’s prophecy: kĕsāʾāh sōlet bĕšeqel ûsātayim śĕʿōrîm bĕšeqel, exactly as predicted.
7.17: wĕhammelek hipqîd ʾet-haššālîš ʾăšer niš’ān ʾal-yāḏô — ‘the king placed the officer on whose hand he had leaned in charge of the gate.’ Niš’ān ʾal-yāḏô (leaned on his hand) — the physical posture of royal support/dependence echoes the idiom of 2 Kgs 5:18 (Naaman leaning on his master’s arm). This officer is the one who mocked the prophecy (v2). The passive wayĕḇûsēhû — ‘the people trampled him’ — fulfills Elisha’s word: seeing but not eating.
7.18: The narrative repeats the prophecy verbatim — kĕdabbēr ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm ʾel-hammelek (as the man of God had spoken to the king). The repetition is the narrator’s verification: the prophetic word was given (v1-2), doubted (v2b), and is now confirmed (vv18-20). The structural feature — prediction, doubt, fulfillment, verification — is typical of the Kings narrator’s method of establishing prophetic credentials.
7.19: The doubting officer’s words are recalled: ʾapōt bĕšāmayim hăyihyeh haddāḇār hazzeh — ‘even if YHWH were to make windows in heaven, could this thing happen?’ The repetition serves as the legal basis for Elisha’s specific judgment (hinnĕkā rōʾeh bĕʿênêkā ûmiššām lōʾ tōʾkēl) — the man who disbelieved the word will see but not benefit. The word rāʾāh (see) connects to the eye-opening theme of chapter 6.
7.20: wayyĕhî-lô kēn — ‘and it happened exactly so to him.’ The terse narrator’s formula (kēn, thus/so) closes the Elisha prophecy narrative with economical precision: the officer was trampled in the gate and died. The waw-consecutive perfect wayyāmot (and he died) is the final word. The narrative circle: prophecy given → doubted → fulfilled → fulfilled exactly. Each link is verbally confirmed by the narrator.

8.1: The chapter opens with wĕʾĕlîšāʿ dibber — ‘Elisha had spoken’ — a pluperfect construction in context (he had previously warned the Shunammite). The reason for urgency: qārāʾ YHWH lārāʿāb — ‘YHWH has called for a famine.’ The Piel of qārāʾ (call, summon) makes YHWH the active agent of the famine — it is a divine summons, not a natural disaster. The seven-year duration (šebaʿ šānîm) echoes Joseph’s seven-year famine (Gen 41:54), framing this as another exodus-type situation requiring prophetic guidance for survival.
8.2: The woman does exactly as the man of God commanded: wattemah wĕtaʿaś kidĕbar ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm — ‘she arose and did according to the word of the man of God.’ The verb wattēšeb bĕʾereṣ pĕlištîm — ‘she lived in the land of the Philistines.’ The Philistine land as a refuge for an Israelite woman is unexpected; it recalls Ruth’s sojourn in Moab. The narrator notes she was there šebaʿ šānîm — seven years, precisely the famine’s duration.
8.3: After seven years: wayyāšob hāʾiššāh mēʾereṣ pĕlištîm — ‘the woman returned.’ The verb šûb (return) is theologically loaded in Kings: it is the call to covenant return (tĕšûḇāh), the hope of restoration. The woman goes to the king to appeal for her house and land: wattēṣēʾ liṣʿōq ʾel-hammelek ʾel-bêtāh wĕʾel-śāḏĕhā — ‘she went out to cry out to the king for her house and land.’ The verb ṣāʿaq (cry out) is again the legal appeal to royal justice.
8.4: The king was talking with Gehazi: wĕhammelek mĕdabbēr ʾel-gēḥăzî naʿar ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm — ‘the king was talking with Gehazi the servant of the man of God.’ The participial construction (mĕdabbēr) marks the scene as simultaneously occurring with the woman’s arrival. Gehazi’s presence here is unexplained — in ch 5:27 he was struck with leprosy permanently. The narrator does not resolve the tension; some interpreters read this as a flashback (before the leprosy episode) or as a different period. The question remains open in the text.
